coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of soybean residue-based nitrogen-chlorine co-doped carbon dots based on deep eutectic solvents: Construction of a PNP fluorescence detection system under the IFE mechanism
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fei
Li, Chen
Li, Yaqian
Wei, Xiao
Yan, Mengwei
Wang, Jiaqi
Gao, Yue
Liu, Jiali
Wang, Yixue
coredata.dc:description: How to manage the potential environmental pollution of waste biomass while realizing the resource utilization of the waste is a topic worthy of in-depth investigation. In this paper, after successfully preparing a deep eutectic solvents, we constructed N, Cl-CDs fluorescent probes with high fluorescence yield and biocompatibility by utilizing waste soybean residue as a cheap nitrogen-rich carbon-sourced biomass precursor. The results showed that the fluorescence quenching of N, Cl-CDs has a good linear relationship with the concentration of p-nitrophenol in the range of 0.1–60 μM, with the detection limit of 30 nM, and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was the inner filter effect. This paper demonstrates that the carbon dots fluorescence system has a superior prospect for the highly selective detection of p-nitrophenol, and provides a reliable basis for the future exploration of efficient and environmentally friendly carbon dots synthetic method as well as the economic recycling of waste.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-chlorine co-doped carbon dots
Soybean residue
Agricultural waste resource utilization
Deep eutectic solvents
P-nitrophenol detection

Synthesis and condition optimization of N, Cl-CDs
Optimization of N, Cl-CDs detection conditions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimization of preparation conditions for N, Cl-CDs: DES composition, water content and soybean residue content.
(a) TEM image of the N, Cl-CDs. (b) Particle size distributions of N, Cl-CDs. (c) TEM high-resolution image of the N, Cl-CDs. (d) XRD patterns of N, Cl-CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of N, Cl-CDs. (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s and (e) Cl2p of high-resolution XPS spectra. (f) FT-IR spectrum of N, Cl-CDs.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N, Cl-CDs under different excitation. (b) UV-vis spectrum and optimal excitation and emission spectra of N, Cl-CDs.

(a) Stability of N, Cl-CDs in aqueous solution. Effect of (b) hydrogen peroxide, (c) sodium chloride, (d) xenon lamp illumination, and (e) ultraviolet light on the fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs.

(a) Effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs. (b) Changes of fluorescence intensity and quenching degree of N, Cl-CDs with concentration. (c) Response time of fluorescence detection of N, Cl-CDs to PNP.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of N, Cl-CDs with different concentrations of PNP. (b) Linear fitting diagram of quantitative detection of PNP by N, Cl-CDs.
Interference of other substances in the detection of PNP by N, Cl-CDs.
(a) UV-vis absorption spectrum of PNP and excitation and emission spectra of N, Cl-CDs. (b) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N, Cl-CDs before and after PNP addition.
Schematic diagram of p-nitrophenol detection mechanism by N, Cl-CDs.
Related parameters of fluorescence attenuation curves.
Detection of PNP in water samples.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of soybean residue-based nitrogen-chlorine co-doped carbon dots based on deep eutectic solvents: Construction of a PNP fluorescence detection system under the IFE mechanism

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: How to manage the potential environmental pollution of waste biomass while realizing the resource utilization of the waste is a topic worthy of in-depth investigation. In this paper, after successfully preparing a deep eutectic solvents, we constructed N, Cl-CDs fluorescent probes with high fluorescence yield and biocompatibility by utilizing waste soybean residue as a cheap nitrogen-rich carbon-sourced biomass precursor. The results showed that the fluorescence quenching of N, Cl-CDs has a good linear relationship with the concentration of p-nitrophenol in the range of 0.1–60 μM, with the detection limit of 30 nM, and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was the inner filter effect. This paper demonstrates that the carbon dots fluorescence system has a superior prospect for the highly selective detection of p-nitrophenol, and provides a reliable basis for the future exploration of efficient and environmentally friendly carbon dots synthetic method as well as the economic recycling of waste.

Nitrogen-chlorine co-doped carbon dots from biomass were prepared greenly and efficiently with the participation of deep eutectic solvents to realize the detection of trace P-nitrophenol concentration in water.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: P-nitrophenol (PNP) is one of the most commonly used phenols in the world and is widely used in leather, dyestuffs, pharmaceuticals, and agricultural production [1,2]. PNP is highly toxic, highly stable, and cumulative in soil and water environment, and its long-term presence poses a great threat to human health and the ecological environment [3]. However, the use of nitrophenolic compounds in the production process is unavoidable to meet the needs of the rapid development of industrial and economic. The pollution caused by PNP can be controlled by the method of detecting the content of PNP in water and taking certain treatment measures. The traditional methods for PNP detection are capillary tube electrophoresis [4], chemiluminescence [5], electrochemical analysis [6], and chromatography [7]. Most of these methods are complicated to operate and expensive in terms of instrumentation, making them uneconomical and impractical for large-scale detection of p-nitrophenol. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a technical method that can sensitively and easily detect trace PNP in water.

Fluorescence spectrometry stands out among several detection methods due to its ease of operation, low detection limit, and high sensitivity, and the fluorophore is a key factor affecting the detection performance of fluorescent sensors. Currently, most materials such as fluorescein [8], rhodamine B [9], coumarin [10], and rare-earth metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [11] are used as fluorescent moieties. In recent years, a newly discovered novel nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs), with the advantages of good fluorescence performance, tunable photoluminescence, water solubility, low toxicity, and biocompatibility, is more attractive than other fluorescent materials [12-16]. However, most of the synthesis methods for CDs have constraints such as time-consuming, complex operation, high cost of carbon source, and not environmentally friendly [17]. Such as Chalmpes et al. [18] used a strong oxidizing agent, HNO3, to prepare carbon dots by reacting with nitrile rubber and hydrazide derivatives, and the synthesis process required the use of strong acids to carbonize small organic molecules, which produced waste water that was not environmentally friendly and has low quantum yields. Therefore, it is a challenge to develop methods that can synthesize carbon dots in a low-cost and large-scale green way. The hydrothermal method is simple to operate and the conditions are easy to adjust, which has obvious advantages among many carbon dots synthesis methods, and natural biomass with wide sources and non-toxicity can generally be chosen as the eco-friendly carbon source precursor for the preparation of CDs [19]. The synthesis of carbon dots from biomass using peppermint [20], wolfberry [21], rose [22], orange juice [23], and spinach [24] as the carbon source precursors has been achieved.

Considering the cost of carbon sources, the environmental pollution and resource waste caused by agricultural waste, preparing carbon dots from waste biomass as a carbon source provides a new idea for the high-value resource utilization of agricultural waste and the economic preparation of carbon dots. Soybean residue (okara) is an insoluble product in industrial processes such as the manufacture of soybean milk, tofu, or soybean oil, and billions of tons of okara are produced every year [25]. Only a small amount of soybean waste residue is utilized as animal feed daily, and most of them are discarded as industrial waste or directly incinerated, which leads to more environmental problems [26,27]. Dry soybean residue is rich in nitrogen and its main components are 50 % dietary fiber, 25 % protein, and 10 % lipid [28], which can be converted into small-sized carbon materials by certain physicochemical methods. Moreover, the heteroatoms and trace elements in soybean dregs can accumulate on the surface of CDs to form functional groups, which can play a good role in modifying CDs. Therefore, soybean residue is a promising biomass carbon source for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanomaterials, which can realize the utilization of waste resources while reducing costs and environmental pollution.

Water is usually used as a green solvent in the hydrothermal preparation of CDs from biomass carbon sources. However, the low solubility of medium and small polar components in water often leaded to problems such as low carbon conversion, which greatly limits the application of biomass CDs in various fields. Although reaction solvents such as toluene [29] and dimethylformamide [30] can obtain CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield, toxicity and volatility limit their use. Therefore, there is a need to find a green solvent that can also improve the carbonation rate and the fluorescence performance of carbon dots while ensuring environmental protection and low cost. Deep eutectic solvents (DES) are new environmentally friendly solvents prepared by combining 2–3 chemical substances through hydrogen bonding [31]. DES have many excellent properties such as high viscosity, strong solvency, low toxicity, and biodegradability, and it can also be used as a dopant, passivator, or modifier [32,33]. DES as a solvents for the preparation of carbon dots not only improves the utilization rate of the carbon source but also serves as a dopant for doping nitrogen, chlorine, and other heteroatoms into CDs to form corresponding covalent bonds to optimize their fluorescence properties [34], which has great advantages in the green synthesis of carbon dots.

Herein, N, Cl-CDs using waste soybean residue as a cheap carbon source were synthesized based on deep eutectic solvents that could realize nitrogen-chlorine synchronous doping as experimental solvents in a green manner, and a novel fluorescent sensor for rapid detection and accurate quantification of PNP was constructed, which developed an eco-friendly pathway to regenerate the value of waste with the potential of large-scale and low-cost preparation of carbon dots from biomass. The resulting N, Cl-CDs exhibited high sensitivity, high stability, and did not require additional chemical modifications. The N, Cl-CDs-based fluorescence system has a wide linear range and low detection limit for the detection of PNP, which provided a new research idea for detecting nitrophenol compounds using carbon dots. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of this fluorescence detection system was obtained by graphical analysis and model data fitting analysis. The preparation of CDs from waste soybean residue in deep eutectic solvents was an economical and reliable method to achieve waste resource utilization, and the determination of trace PNP in real water samples was very effective, which was in line with the sustainable development strategy.

The okara used in this experiment was obtained from a local soymilk store in China (Xi'an). Most of the drugs were provided by Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory: acetamide (CH3CONH2), glycerol (C3H8O3), quinine sulfate (C20H24N2O2)2·H2SO4·2H2O, concentrated sulfuric acid, resorcinol (C6H6O2), catechol (C6H6O2), hydroquinone (C6H6O2), and dichlorophenol (C6H4Cl2O). P-nitrophenol (C6H5NO3), urea (CH4N2O), malic acid (C4H6O5), choline chloride (C5H14ClNO), m-phenylenediamine (C6H8N2), and p-phenylenediamine (C6H8N2) were purchased from Aladdin Bio-chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Malonic acid (C3H4O4), tetrabutylammonium bromide (C16H36BrN), hydrogen peroxide were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid, sodium chloride (Henan Haohua Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), and sodium hydroxide (Nanjing DuLai Biotechnology Co., Ltd.). All the experimental reagents were analytically pure.

The morphological characteristics of the carbon dots were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TecnaiG2 F20, USA) and X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku ultimaIV). The surface structure of the carbon dots was analyzed using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Spectrum Two, USA) and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha, USA). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained on an Evolution 220 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Fluorescence spectra were obtained by F97 XP fluorescence spectrophotometer (Lengguang, China). Fluorescence decay curves were recorded using an Edinburgh FLS9 transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of soybean dregs-based N, Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: DES were synthesized by oil bath heating. HBA (choline chloride and tetrabutylammonium bromide) and HBD (urea, malonic acid, acetamide, glycerol, malic acid) were mixed in specific molar ratios, and heated to 80 °C in an oil bath with vigorous stirring until a homogeneous eutectic mixture of about 15 mL was formed. The optimal DES for the preparation of CDs was obtained after several comparative experiments: the molar ratio of choline chloride (6.981 g) and urea (9.009 g) was 1:3.

N, Cl-CDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method. 0.06 g of soybean residue was added to 10 mL of DES and stirred well. Then, the mixture was put into a 50 mL PTFE-lined high-pressure reactor with the temperature and time adjusted to 200 °C and 10 h. The hydrothermal reaction was carried out in an electrically heated blast drying oven. At the end of the reaction, the solution was cooled to room temperature and the high viscosity carbon dots solution was diluted to 30 mL with deionized water and filtered. The pure N, Cl-CDs mother liquor was further obtained by dialysis through a dialysis membrane (3000 Da) for 48 h. Finally, the carbon dots with high fluorescence quantum yield were obtained by optimizing the type and water content of DES, the amount of soybean residue, the reaction time and the reaction temperature.

The fluorescence quantum yield of the quantum dots was measured to be 26.74 % at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm using quinine sulfate (dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a reference. The fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of N, Cl-CDs was calculated using the formula:
(1)
φ X
=
φ
S T
(
k X
k
S T
)
(
η X 2
η
S T
2
)
where "X" is the sample, "ST" is the standard reference, "k" is the slope of the absorbance over the integrated area of the fluorescence spectrum, and "η" is the refractive index.
(2) Fluorescence characteristic detection

The N, Cl-CDs mother liquor was diluted 104 times with deionized water, and the PNP standard solution with the concentration of 1.0 M was prepared and stored at 4.0 °C. The diluted N, Cl-CDs solution was placed in a standard colorimetric tube, mixed with different amounts of PNP standard solution and volume fixed to 10 mL, then moved to a cuvette to measure its fluorescence intensity. In this experiment, the fluorescence spectrophotometer was set as: excitation wavelength of 330 nm, emission wavelength range of 350–600 nm, excitation wavelength slit width of 10 nm, emission wavelength slit width of 10 nm, photomultiplier tube voltage of 800 V. Three parallel experiments were performed in the detection process, and the results were averaged.

The constructed N, Cl-CDs fluorescence sensor was used to determine the PNP content in general tap water samples to verify its practicability. Since no PNP was detected in the original samples, different concentrations of PNP standard solutions were added to the water samples for spiking and recovery experiments. The spiked water samples were mixed with N, Cl-CDs solutions and stabilized for fluorescence detection, and the PNP concentrations were calculated from the fitted linear equations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and condition optimization of N, Cl-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, waste soybean residue was utilized as a biomass carbon source precursor to reduce the cost, and deep eutectic solvents were added to complete the surface modification of carbon dots for green synthesis of N, Cl-CDs possessing excellent fluorescence properties. As shown in Scheme 1 , after the addition of soybean residue to DES and transferring it to an autoclave for high-temperature hydrothermal reaction, the soybean residue was fully carbonized and the insoluble small molecules were further dissolved. At the same time, DES modifies the carbon dots to form blue fluorescent N, Cl-CDs with heteroatom doping and higher fluorescence quantum yield, and finally, the fluorescence of N, Cl-CDs was effectively quenched by adding PNP.

Based on the structural designability of DES, the type and molar ratio of DES precursors were optimized by modulation to provide sufficient reaction sites and targets for the preparation of quantum dots [35]. In this experiment, different HBA (choline chloride and tetrabutylammonium bromide) and HBD (urea, malic acid, malonic acid, glycerol and acetamide) were used to synthesize DES and further prepare CDs after modulating the ratio of the components. According to the experimental data, the most significant effect of improving the FQY of quantum dots was the combination of choline chloride and urea. In this experiment, only the effect of liquid DES on the preparation of CDs was investigated, and the proportion of water in the DES also affects the synthesis of CDs. Therefore, optimal experiments were carried out for the solvent water content, the proportion of DES components and the amount of soybean residue. As shown in Fig. 1 , the highest quantum yield of N, Cl-CDs of 26.74 % was achieved by preparing DES with 0 water content as solvent and adding 0.06 g of soybean residue under hydrothermal conditions at 200 °C for 10 h with a molar ratio of HBA (choline chloride) and HBD (urea) of 1:3.

The microscopic morphology of N, Cl-CDs was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 , the N, Cl-CDs were spherical structures with particle sizes ranging from 4.5 to 7.5 nm, with an average size of 5.77 nm, and were uniformly distributed in water with a lattice spacing of 0.297 nm. The internal structures of the N, Cl-CDs were further analyzed by X-ray diffractometry. N, Cl-CDs have broad characteristic absorption peaks around 2θ=25.9° (Fig. 2d), which were consistent with the characteristic diffraction peaks of amorphous carbon and correspond to the (002) crystal surface of graphite. Thus, the synthesized N, Cl-CDs have the amorphous carbon structure and graphitic properties.

The elemental species and chemical bonding of N, Cl-CDs were analyzed by XPS and the results were shown in Fig. 3 . Typical peaks of four elements were obtained from the full-range XPS analysis spectrum in Fig. 3a: C1s (285.25 eV), N1s (400.69 eV), Cl2p (197.96 eV), and O1s (531.39 eV), and the corresponding percentages of each element were: 63.99 %, 18.58 %, 10.42 %, and 7.02 %. In addition, analysis of the high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 3b) showed four peaks at 284.06 eV, 284.80 eV, 286.21 eV and 288.65 eV, corresponding to CC, C=C, CO/CCl/CN, and OC=O; and the high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 3c) showed two peaks at 399.62 eV (CN), 401.67 eV (NH) peaks; the high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 3d) corresponded to three peaks at 531.26 eV (CO), 532.44 eV (C=O), 533.31 eV (-COOH); the high-resolution spectrum of Cl2p (Fig. 3e) with peaks at 197.60 eV, 199.25 eV, and 200.10 eV corresponds to Cl2p3/2, Cl2p, and Cl2p1/2, respectively [36-38].

The structures of the functional groups on the surface of N, Cl-CDs can be qualitatively analyzed by using infrared spectroscopy. As can be seen in Fig. 3f, the stretching vibration of NH induces the formation of absorption peaks at 3500–3100 cm-1, the strong absorption peak at 1652 cm-1 is related to the stretching vibration of olefin C=C, the stretching vibration of CN induces strong peaks at 1350 cm-1–1000 cm-1, and the stretching vibration of CCl contributes to the appearance of absorption peaks at 758 cm-1. XPS and FT-IR characterization analyses confirmed that the N and Cl atoms in DES (choline chloride and urea) were successfully doped into carbon dots, and the corresponding functional groups, such as CN, CCl, and NH, existed on the surfaces of N, Cl-CDs.

The optical properties of N, Cl-CDs were analyzed using fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis spectroscopy. The photoluminescence properties of carbon dots are different from those of conventional semiconductor quantum dots, as shown in Fig. 4 a, the emission of soybean dregs-based carbon dots was affected by the excitation state, and the emission spectrum was broad and extends to larger wavelengths as the intensity decreases rapidly. This excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence behavior may be caused by the π*→n jump of the surface functional groups (C=C/CO/CN/CCl) of the N, Cl-CDs, as deduced from the analysis. As shown in Fig. 4b, N, Cl-CDs exhibit a blue emission of 391 nm under excitation at the optimal excitation wavelength of 330 nm.

N, Cl-CDs stability assay experiments are a necessary step in determining their viability for practical applications. Therefore, the fluorescence stability of N, Cl-CDs was investigated in aqueous solution, hydrogen peroxide, normal saline, or by xenon lamp and ultraviolet lamp irradiation.

The fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs in aqueous solution was examined several times within a certain time. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the N, Cl-CDs were stably dispersed in aqueous solution, and their fluorescence intensities were basically unchanged within 120 min.

As shown in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs decreased with the increase of H2O2 concentration, and the decrease of fluorescence intensity was more than 50 % when H2O2 reached 2.0 mol/L. However, as shown in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs was always maintained at about 90 % under 0–2.0 mol/L NaCl solution. As shown in Fig. 5d, after continuous irradiation with xenon lamp for 210 min, N, Cl-CDs showed a lower degree of photobleaching phenomenon, and the fluorescence intensity remained above 80 %; under the irradiation of UV lamp, the fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs decreased with the increase of irradiation time, but always remained above 75 % (Fig. 5e). All the above experimental results indicate that N, Cl-CDs have good fluorescence stability and were feasible in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of N, Cl-CDs detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain the optimal detection environment, the fluorescence performance of carbon dots fluorescent systems was investigated in this study at different solution concentrations, pH and detection times.

As shown in Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs fluctuates less in acidic environment, and the fluorescence intensity was highest at pH = 7, which was the best detection condition. When the pH reached 7–12, the high alkaline environment prompted the changes of amino and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of N, Cl-CDs, which will destroy or prevent the electron leaps of some defects in the CDs, making them easy to be protonated and deprotonated, and the fluorescence intensity decreases rapidly. Therefore, N, Cl-CDs were more sensitive to alkaline environment, and Cl doping was easy to form hydrogen bonds.

Solution concentration plays an important role in influencing the fluorescence intensity and quenching efficiency of fluorescent sensors. The fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs at different concentrations were measured by fluorescence spectrometer, and the quenching efficiency of different concentrations of N, Cl-CDs were investigated at 30 μM concentration of PNP solution to obtain the optimal detection concentration of N, Cl-CDs fluorescence sensor. As shown in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs increased with its concentration, but its quenching efficiency decreased with the increase of concentration. Because the fluorescence intensity of the sensor is directly determined by the number of carbon dots per unit volume, the number of carbon dots per unit volume increases when the concentration is higher, and the fluorescence intensity is higher. However, the number of carbon dots quenching by a certain concentration of PNP is constant, so the quenching efficiency is lower when the number of carbon dots per unit volume is higher. Therefore, in order to balance the fluorescence intensity and the quenching efficiency to obtain the optimal experimental results, 100 μL of the mother liquor was dissolved in 1 L of deionized water as the optimal detection concentration. In addition, it takes time for the fluorescence reaction to stabilize when the carbon dots come into contact with the target pollutant. As shown in Fig. 6c, in this experiment, the fluorescence emission intensity decreased rapidly after the addition of PNP (30 μM) to the sensor solution and stabilized after 15 s. Therefore, 15 s was the optimal detection time for this experiment.

N, Cl-CDs fluorescent sensors can be used to detect PNP due to their good optical properties, and the ability of N, Cl-CDs to quantitatively detect PNP was investigated under the optimal detection conditions obtained from the above experiments. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the blue fluorescence of N, Cl-CDs in visible light and 330 nm UV light was rapidly quenching after the addition of PNP, and the degree of fluorescence quenching of N, Cl-CDs increased with the increase of PNP concentration. The relationship between fluorescence intensity and PNP concentration was fitted with the Stern-Volmer equation:
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
S V
[ C ]
Where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs in the absence of PNP in the solution, and F is the fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of PNP. Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [C] represents the concentration of the target. As shown in Fig. 7b, the two showed a good linear relationship under the optimal detection conditions, with a linear range of 0.1 μM-60 μM and a correlation coefficient of R2=0.99797, and the fitting equation was F0/F = 0.0415402*C(PNP)+0.994413. Finally, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 30 nM.

In order to better investigate the specificity of N, Cl-CDs in detecting PNP, several common contaminants (CT, Resorcinol, HQ, MPD, PPD, DCP) were selected as interfering contaminants to carry out the experiments, so as to verify their anti-interference ability and selectivity. Analyzing Fig. 8 , it can be seen that the other interfering substances have almost no effect on the fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs, and only the experimental group containing PNP caused a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of N, Cl-CDs, resulting in the fluorescence quenching phenomenon. Therefore, the N, Cl-CDs fluorescence sensing system has good selectivity and anti-interference ability in the detection of PNP.

The sensing recognition mechanism of carbon dots fluorescence sensors usually utilizes the chemical/physical interactions between the target and the carbon dots resulting in the quenching of the carbon dots fluorescence to achieve the detection of the target. The detection and identification of contaminants by fluorescent sensors can be caused by one or more mechanisms, such as photogenerated electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), and dynamic quenching or static quenching.

The inner filter effect is due to the attenuation of the excitation beam in the detection system, and the absorption of the excitation or emission radiation of the fluorophore by the fluorophore or absorber in excess concentration in the solution [39,40]. There is no intermolecular interaction between the absorber and the fluorophore in solution, so the absorption spectrum of the absorber in the IFE mechanism will overlap with the excitation or emission band of the fluorophore, and the absorption peak of the fluorophore will not change [41]. In addition, FRET, often referred to as fluorescence resonance energy transfer, occurs when the fluorescence spectrum of CDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of the quencher, and a FRET effect occurs between the excited state CDs and the ground state quencher [42]. In this experiment, the fluorescence quenching mechanism was first analyzed by studying the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the fluorescent sensor and the UV-vis absorption spectrum of p-nitrophenol. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of PNP strongly overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of N, Cl-CDs. So the fluorescence quenching mechanism could be fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE).

Although the FRET process and the IFE process will produce a high degree of overlap between the fluorescence emission spectrum of the fluorophore and the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of the acceptor, the FRET process is controlled by molecular proximity distances, whereas the IFE process is affected by radiative energy transfer, and energetic perturbations of the donor occur mainly in the ground state. Therefore, the IFE process does not change the fluorescence lifetime, but only reduces its fluorescence intensity. In this study, we used time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) to record the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N, Cl-CDs before and after the addition of acceptor PNP to further verify the fluorescence quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. 9b, the fluorescence decay curves before and after PNP addition almost overlapped, but the addition of PNP did not change the fluorescence lifetimes of N, Cl-CDs. The biexponential model was used to fit the fluorescence lifetime curves:
(3)
τ
a v e
=
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
Where A denotes the amplitude and τ is the fluorescence lifetime, the equation can be simplified as:
(4)
τ
a v e
=
τ 1
R e
l 1
% +
τ 2
R e
l 2
%

The transient fluorescence lifetime parameters were obtained by data fitting, and then the reliability of the fitting results was verified by using the confidence factor χ2, which is higher when χ2 is closer to one. As shown in Table 1 , the average fluorescence lifetime of N, Cl-CDs was 20.66 ns when no PNP was added. The average fluorescence lifetime of N, Cl-CDs after the addition of PNP was 21.03 ns, which was basically unchanged.

Based on the analysis of the UV-vis absorption spectrum of PNP with the excitation and emission spectra of N, Cl-CDs, and the fitting of the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N, Cl-CDs before and after the addition of PNP, it was confirmed that the quenching mechanism of the PNP fluorescence sensors in this study was the inner filter effect. As shown in Scheme 2 , in this study, p-nitrophenol was used as the absorber to trigger the inner filter effect, which led to the fluorescence quenching of N, Cl-CDs, thus realizing the detection of PNP concentration in water.

In order to evaluate the feasibility of fluorescence detection of PNP by N, Cl-CDs in practical applications, it was used for the detection of PNP in tap water samples. Since PNP in tap water samples was almost undetectable, spiked recovery experiments were performed by adding different concentrations of PNP to tap water samples. The PNP spiked water samples with concentrations in the range of 5.0–50 μM were detected, and the results were shown in Table 2 . The recoveries of the spiked water samples ranged from 97.4 % to 104.2 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were 1.9 %−7.0 %. The experimental results show that N, Cl-CDs fluorescence probe can sensitively detect PNP in actual samples.

Herein, nitrogen-chlorine co-doped biomass carbon dots were prepared for the first time by using soybean residue as the precursor of biomass carbon source with the participation of green deep eutectic solvents, and a novel fluorescent sensor for N, Cl-CDs based on IFE mechanism was designed to complete the accurate detection of the concentration of trace PNP in water. The innovative design of this experiment using the waste soybean residue and DES realizes the effective control and resource utilization of the potential environmental hazards of agricultural waste, solves the problems of high cost, poor fluorescence performance, and the need for additional dopant of the general carbon dots preparation method. In addition, under the optimal detection conditions, the fluorescence of N, Cl-CDs can be effectively quenched by PNP, thus realizing the sensitive detection of PNP with the detection limit as low as 30 nM, which can be stably used for the determination of PNP in real water samples and verified the quenching mechanism as inner filter effect. This design provides a new strategy for low-cost green synthesis of biomass fluorescent probes and PNP detection methods in water, which has great potential for application in the fields of value regeneration of waste organisms and environmental monitoring.

Fei Wang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Chen Li: Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yaqian Li: Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Xiao Wei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Mengwei Yan: Validation, Resources, Data curation. Jiaqi Wang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Yue Gao: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Jiali Liu: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Yixue Wang: Validation, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. And this study did not involve human or animal samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research has been supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (42207101 and 42220104005), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, CHD (300102293208).
